5-5.title: Freedom Riders Depart Washington D.C. on First Interstate Journey
5-5.summary: On May 5, 1961, thirteen Freedom Riders — seven Black and six white — departed Washington D.C. aboard two interstate buses organized by CORE (Congress of Racial Equality) to challenge the non-enforcement of Boynton v. Virginia (1960), which had outlawed segregation in interstate bus terminals. Led by CORE director James Farmer, the riders deliberately sat in defiance of Jim Crow seating customs, knowing federal law was on their side but that Southern states refused compliance. The journey was designed to provoke a federal response by exposing the gap between Supreme Court rulings and ground-level enforcement.
5-5.chain: The violent mob attacks on Freedom Riders in Anniston and Birmingham, Alabama days later forced the Kennedy administration to issue Interstate Commerce Commission regulations in September 1961 formally desegregating all interstate travel facilities — establishing the precedent that sustained federal enforcement, not court rulings alone, was required to dismantle legal segregation.
